questions.question: Explain how experimental evidence supports the idea that matter is composed of tiny particles.
questions.answer: Experiments such as grinding chalk into powder and dissolving sugar in water show that substances can be divided into smaller particles while maintaining their properties, indicating that matter is made of tiny particles.

questions.question: Analyze how the sugar dissolving experiment demonstrates both the existence of particles and interparticle spaces.
questions.answer: When sugar dissolves in water, the sugar particles spread throughout the solution. Since the total volume does not increase significantly, this shows that sugar particles occupy spaces between water particles.
answer_outline: Particles occupy spaces between water particles

questions.question: Explain the role of interparticle forces in determining the state of matter.
questions.answer: Interparticle forces determine how strongly particles are held together. Strong forces produce solids, moderate forces produce liquids, and very weak forces produce gases.

questions.question: Compare the arrangement and movement of particles in solids, liquids, and gases.
questions.answer: In solids, particles are tightly packed and vibrate in fixed positions. In liquids, particles are loosely packed and move within a limited space. In gases, particles are far apart and move freely in all directions.

questions.question: Explain why solids are rigid while liquids and gases are not.
questions.answer: Solids are rigid because their particles are tightly packed and strongly attracted, preventing movement. Liquids and gases have weaker forces, allowing particles to move more freely.
answer_outline: Weak attraction in liquids and gases

questions.question: Explain how heating affects particle motion in solids.
questions.answer: Heating increases the kinetic energy of particles, causing them to vibrate faster. When the vibration becomes strong enough, the particles overcome attractive forces and the solid melts into a liquid.

questions.question: Explain the process of melting and the significance of melting point.
questions.answer: Melting occurs when a solid is heated and its particles gain enough energy to overcome attractive forces. The temperature at which this occurs is called the melting point.

questions.question: Explain how the particle model accounts for the ability of liquids to flow.
questions.answer: Liquids can flow because their particles are loosely packed and can move around each other within a limited space.

questions.question: Analyze why gases occupy the entire available space.
questions.answer: Gas particles have negligible attraction and large spaces between them. This allows them to move freely and spread throughout the available space.

questions.question: Explain how the gas jar experiment demonstrates the spreading of gases.
questions.answer: Smoke collected in one gas jar spreads into another jar when the separating plate is removed. This demonstrates that gas particles move freely and mix with other gases.

questions.question: Explain how the syringe experiment proves that gases are compressible.
questions.answer: When the plunger of a syringe is pushed while blocking the nozzle, the air inside compresses and the volume decreases, showing that gas particles can be pushed closer together.

questions.question: Explain why liquids are nearly incompressible compared to gases.
questions.answer: Liquids have very little space between particles compared to gases. Therefore particles cannot be pushed much closer together.

questions.question: Explain how the potassium permanganate experiment demonstrates particle motion.
questions.answer: When potassium permanganate crystals are added to water, the purple color spreads gradually without stirring. This shows that particles are constantly moving.

questions.question: Explain why diffusion occurs faster at higher temperatures.
questions.answer: Higher temperatures increase particle kinetic energy, causing particles to move faster and spread more quickly.

questions.question: Explain why diffusion occurs faster in gases than in liquids.
questions.answer: Diffusion occurs faster in gases because gas particles have larger spaces between them and move more rapidly.

questions.question: Explain the scientific reason why perfume smell spreads quickly in a room.
questions.answer: Perfume molecules mix with air particles and spread through diffusion due to the continuous motion of gas particles.

questions.question: Explain the difference between evaporation and boiling.
questions.answer: Evaporation occurs at the surface of a liquid at temperatures below the boiling point, while boiling occurs throughout the liquid at a fixed temperature called the boiling point.

questions.question: Explain why liquids have a definite volume but no definite shape.
questions.answer: Liquid particles are close together but can move around each other, allowing them to take the shape of their container while maintaining volume.

questions.question: Explain why gases have neither fixed shape nor fixed volume.
questions.answer: Gas particles move freely in all directions and occupy the entire space available due to weak attractive forces.

questions.question: Explain the role of particle motion in everyday phenomena.
questions.answer: Particle motion explains processes such as dissolving sugar in water, spreading perfume, and evaporation of liquids.

questions.question: Explain how soap molecules remove oily stains using particle interaction.
questions.answer: Soap molecules attach to oil particles on one side and water molecules on the other, allowing the oil to mix with water and be washed away.

questions.question: Explain how particle theory helps explain changes of state.
questions.answer: Changes of state occur when particles gain or lose energy, causing changes in particle motion and attractive forces.

questions.question: Explain why sand does not dissolve in water while sugar does.
questions.answer: Sugar dissolves because its particles can mix with water particles, while sand particles are insoluble and settle at the bottom.

questions.question: Explain how the particulate nature of matter helps scientists understand physical changes.
questions.answer: Physical changes involve rearrangement of particles without changing the substance itself.

questions.question: Summarize the particle model explaining solids, liquids, and gases.
questions.answer: The particle model explains that solids have tightly packed particles with strong attraction, liquids have moderately spaced particles with limited movement, and gases have widely spaced particles moving freely.
